perturbation.original_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties for five (5) years following the date of its disclosure by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as provided below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided Receiving Party shall give Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Receiving Party shall not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties for one (1) year following the date of its disclosure by Disclosing Party to Receiving Party, except to Receiving Party's consultants as provided below. However, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information in accordance with judicial or other governmental order, provided Receiving Party shall give Disclosing Party reasonable notice prior to such disclosure and shall comply with any applicable protective order or equivalent.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Washington state law, which acknowledges the potential for longer protection periods depending on the nature of the trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.010 defines "trade secret" broadly and does not limit protection to a single year. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent and purpose of the Washington Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The changed contract text reduces the confidentiality period for trade secrets from five years to one year. This contradicts the intent of RCW 19.108.010, which defines 'trade secret' without limiting the protection period to one year. While the law doesn't specify a minimum protection period, reducing it to one year may be deemed insufficient, potentially undermining the protection afforded to trade secrets under the Washington Trade Secrets Act. Both scraped snippets define 'trade secret' and 'misappropriation' under RCW 19.108.010, highlighting the law's intent to protect valuable confidential information. A one-year protection period may not adequately safeguard information meeting the definition of a trade secret, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (g) If either party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington. Process may be served on either party by U.S. Mail, postage prepaid, certified or registered, return receipt requested, or by such other method as is authorized by the Washington Long Arm Statute.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) If either party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees, regardless of the outcome. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and both parties further consent to jurisdiction by the state and federal courts sitting in the State of Washington. Process may be served on either party by U.S. Mail, postage prepaid, certified or registered, return receipt requested, or by such other method as is authorized by the Washington Long Arm Statute.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the prevailing party's right to recover attorney's fees may conflict with Washington state law, which allows for fee recovery in certain contract disputes or under specific statutes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington law regarding attorney's fees in contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 states that in any action on a contract or lease, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party designated in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees and costs. By stating that each party bears its own fees, it contradicts this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts RCW 4.84.330, as evidenced by both provided snippets. The original text allowed the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute. The changed text, however, states that 'each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees, regardless of the outcome.' RCW 4.84.330 explicitly states that any contract provision waiving attorneys' fees for the prevailing party is void. The modified contract creates a waiver of attorney's fees, therefore contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, to keep confidential the Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Material only to Receiving Party's employees or consultants on a need-to-know basis. Receiving Party will have executed or shall execute appropriate written agreements with its employees and consultants sufficient to enable it to comply with all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving Party shall take security precautions it deems adequate to protect Confidential Information. Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information or Confidential Material only to Receiving Party's employees or consultants. Receiving Party is not required to execute written agreements with its employees and consultants.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written agreements with employees and consultants to protect confidential information may not satisfy the 'reasonable measures' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.010(4)(b) defines "misappropriation" and implies a duty to maintain secrecy.  The modified text eliminates a key measure for maintaining secrecy (written agreements), which could jeopardize trade secret status. This contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for written agreements with employees and consultants, which the perturbation explanation argues undermines the 'reasonable measures' standard to protect trade secrets under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act. RCW 19.108.010 defines 'misappropriation,' implying a duty to maintain secrecy. By eliminating the written agreement requirement, the modified text weakens the measures taken to maintain secrecy, creating a contradiction. The original text mandated 'appropriate written agreements,' while the changed text states, 'Receiving Party is not required to execute written agreements,' which is a direct contradiction impacting the maintenance of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive or equitable relief as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages will be the sole remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information, and Disclosing Party waives all rights to injunctive or equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Waiving the right to injunctive relief may be unenforceable under Washington law if it effectively eliminates a key remedy for trade secret misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the court's inherent power to grant equitable relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington law regarding equitable remedies for trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108.020 specifically allows for injunctive relief for actual or threatened misappropriation. While parties can contractually limit remedies, a complete waiver of injunctive relief, particularly in cases of trade secret theft, may be viewed as against public policy and unenforceable, as it undermines the core protection the statute offers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.020: Remedies for misappropriation—Injunction, royalty. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.020 19.108.010 << 19.108.020 >> 19.108.030 PDF RCW 19.108.020 Remedies for misappropriation — Injunction, royalty. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. [ 1981 c 286 s 2 .]

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts RCW 19.108.020, as highlighted in both scraped law snippets. The original text allowed for injunctive relief, whereas the changed text waives all rights to injunctive or equitable relief for the Disclosing Party. RCW 19.108.020(1) states, 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' By waiving the right to injunctive relief, the changed text removes a remedy explicitly provided by the statute, creating a clear conflict with the law.
